notable.notable_blurb: First U.S. Under Secretary of State, 1919

institutions.institution_blurb: Awarded D.C.L. honorary degree from the University of the South in 1928 — grandson of co-founder Bishop Polk.

military_service.service_record: Served with Troop A, New York Volunteer Cavalry, April 1898. Appointed Captain and assistant on Quartermaster Staff of General Ernst's Brigade, June 19, 1898. Stationed at Chickamauga, GA, Charleston, SC, and in Puerto Rico. Honorably discharged November 30, 1899.
military_service.unit: Troop A, New York Volunteer Cavalry; later Quartermaster Staff, General Ernst's Brigade

narrative_blocks.header: First US Under Secretary of State, 1919-1920
narrative_blocks.body: When the office of U.S. Under Secretary of State was created in 1919, Frank Lyon Polk became its first occupant — serving July 1, 1919 to June 15, 1920. He briefly served as Acting Secretary of State from February 14 to March 12, 1920, between Robert Lansing and Bainbridge Colby. He had previously served as the 4th Counselor of the State Department from September 16, 1915 through June 30, 1919.

narrative_blocks.header: Headed American delegation at Paris Peace Conference
narrative_blocks.body: After President Wilson and Secretary Lansing departed Paris in 1919, Polk headed the American Commission to Negotiate Peace and represented the United States at the remaining Paris Peace Conference sessions. He was made a Grand Officer of the French Legion of Honor and elected to the North Carolina Society of the Cincinnati in 1919. His portrait was painted by Sir Oswald Birley in 1923.

narrative_blocks.header: Name partner of Davis Polk & Wardwell law firm
narrative_blocks.body: Frank Lyon Polk's surname is the 'Polk' in Davis Polk & Wardwell — one of the elite New York law firms still bearing his name today. He had managed John W. Davis's 1924 Democratic presidential campaign; Davis was a fellow Davis Polk name partner. From 1932 until his death in 1943 he served as President of the New York Public Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived attempted mayoral assassination, 1914
narrative_blocks.body: On April 17, 1914, while serving as Corporation Counsel for New York City, Polk was wounded by gunfire when a former city employee attempted to assassinate Mayor John Purroy Mitchel. The mayor survived; Polk recovered from his gunshot wound. The episode preceded by sixteen months his appointment as Counselor to the State Department.

He was made a Grand Officer of the French Legion of Honor and elected to the North Carolina Society of the Cincinnati in 1919. His portrait was painted by Sir Oswald Birley in 1923.

Davis's 1924 Democratic presidential campaign; Davis was a fellow Davis Polk name partner. From 1932 until his death in 1943 he served as President of the New York Public Library.

naming_inspiration.note: Named for his late paternal uncle John Metcalfe Polk Sr. (HD5055, 1875-1904) who died of autopsy-acquired pneumonia at 28, four years before HD5048's 1908 birth.

narrative_blocks.header: Pinchas Zukerman's godmother; gave him first violin
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Sturgis Polk Guest was godmother to the celebrated Israeli-American violinist Pinchas Zukerman, and gave him his first violin. She supported the Dance Theater of Harlem, the Joffrey Ballet, and the Metropolitan Opera Association. She also founded the Musicians Emergency Fund.

notable.notable_blurb: Ambassador to Ireland 1965-1969; Epsom Derby winner twice

narrative_blocks.header: U.S. Ambassador to Ireland, 1965-1969
narrative_blocks.body: President Lyndon B. Johnson appointed Raymond Guest Sr. as U.S. Ambassador to Ireland in 1965, where he served until 1969. He had earlier served in the Virginia State Senate as a Democrat from 1947 to 1953. He was a director of Bessemer Securities of New York and a trustee of the Virginia Museum of Fine Arts.

narrative_blocks.header: Twice won Epsom Derby; 1975 Grand National winner
narrative_blocks.body: Raymond Guest won the Epsom Derby in 1962 (Larkspur) and 1968 (Sir Ivor, who also took the Washington DC International that year). His Tom Rolfe won the 1965 Preakness Stakes. In 1975 his L'Escargot won the Grand National at Aintree — perhaps the most difficult horse race in the world — having already twice won the Cheltenham Cup.

narrative_blocks.header: WWII Navy; Legion of Merit and Bronze Star
narrative_blocks.body: Guest served in the U.S. Navy during World War II — in the Atlantic, on minesweepers, and in Europe. He was briefly aide to John Gilbert Winant, U.S. Ambassador at the Court of St. James. His decorations included the Legion of Merit and Bronze Star (both with combat V) plus decorations from Britain, France, Denmark, and Norway.

notable.notable_blurb: 28-year Virginia Delegate; state park named while living

death.cause: cancer (non-Hodgkin lymphoma); died at Rock Hill Farm

military_service.note: Took year off from Yale to serve in Marine Corps Reserves between Yale Class of 1963 and Class of 1964 (graduated with latter). Cold War era; no specific war. Proudly sported Marine Corps tattoo on upper arm.

narrative_blocks.header: Virginia House of Delegates 1972-2000; minority leader
narrative_blocks.body: Andy Guest served seven terms (28 years, 1972-2000) in the Virginia House of Delegates as a Republican from Warren County. He served as House Minority Leader from December 1985 to November 1991 — a pioneering Republican in an era when his party held few seats in Richmond. He championed pari-mutuel betting (his 1988 bill led to the Colonial Downs racetrack) and land-and-wildlife conservation throughout his tenure.

narrative_blocks.header: State park named for him while still living
narrative_blocks.body: In 1995 the Commonwealth of Virginia named a state park in his honor: the Shenandoah River Raymond R. 'Andy' Guest Jr. State Park, on the south fork of the Shenandoah River in Warren County near his Rock Hill Farm home. His Yale '64 classmate Chris Getman noted that Andy was 'the only person in history to have a national park named after him while still living.' He and his wife Mary Scott put easements on Rock Hill Farm and bequeathed its 3.5 miles of riverfront to the state.

narrative_blocks.header: Voted from hospital chemo isolation, 1994 session
narrative_blocks.body: Diagnosed with non-Hodgkin lymphoma in 1996 and given six months to live, Guest underwent a stem-cell transplant he believed extended his life by five years. During earlier 1994 chemo isolation he missed 35 days of the General Assembly session but the legislature set up a system to enable him to witness floor proceedings and to vote remotely from his hospital bed — a striking testament to his bipartisan respect. He sponsored four bills and budget amendments from isolation that session.

burial.place: Memorial Cemetery of Saint John's Church, Laurel Hollow, Nassau County NY

marriages.notes: Also mother of Winthrop III, Alice, James, and Linda Rutherfurd — not yet built as separate entries.

burial.place: Tranquility Cemetery, Green Township, Sussex County, NJ

narrative_blocks.header: Lived 91 years; ten grandchildren, twelve great-grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: Alice Potter Polk Rutherfurd died at 91 in New York City in 2009, twenty-one years after her husband Winthrop Rutherfurd. She left five children — including her son Lewis Polk Rutherfurd, the Hong Kong venture capitalist — plus ten grandchildren and twelve great-grandchildren at her death. White roses were placed on her casket at the Church of the Heavenly Rest in New York.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mother: Alice Kearny Morton Rutherfurd (1879-1917) — daughter of Levi P. Morton, VP under Harrison; died of appendicitis age 38 leaving 6 young children. Not built as separate entry.

marriages.notes: Also fathered Winthrop III, Alice, James, Linda Rutherfurd — not yet built.
3 children: Lewis Stuyvesant Rutherfurd, Andrew Hugh Auchincloss Rutherfurd, Alexandra Rutherfurd — not yet built.

notable.notable_blurb: Hong Kong venture capitalist; expanded McDonald's to Asia

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Inter-Asia Venture Management, Hong Kong 1972
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis Polk Rutherfurd moved to Hong Kong after his 1966 marriage and founded Inter-Asia Venture Management Ltd in 1972 — raising $1 million for his first fund and completing fifteen investments. In 1974 he led the expansion of McDonald's into Hong Kong. He has remained co-founder and Managing Director of the firm for over fifty years, serving also as Vice President of the American Chamber of Commerce in Hong Kong.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Jackie Kennedy's half-sister Janet Auchincloss
narrative_blocks.body: On July 30, 1966 Lewis married Janet Jennings Auchincloss — the half-sister of First Lady Jacqueline Lee Bouvier Kennedy Onassis. They had three children before Janet's death from cancer in 1985 at age forty. The marriage made him brother-in-law to Jackie Kennedy through her mother Janet Lee Auchincloss Bouvier's second marriage to Hugh Auchincloss.

narrative_blocks.header: Earned Doctor of Business Administration at age 70
narrative_blocks.body: In 2015 at age 70, Lewis Polk Rutherfurd completed a Doctor of Business Administration at City University of Hong Kong. His thesis examined pre-deal processes in Asian venture capital funds from 1980 to 2000 — drawing on his own four decades pioneering the field. Inter-Asia Venture Management now oversees five private equity funds focused on China, India, Thailand, Singapore, and Indonesia.

sources.short: Online vital records; The Record LXXI; EH 1909

death.cause: 1st Lt U.S. Army Reserve Medical Corps at death; volunteered for WWI service in 1917 at age seventy-two

marriages.notes: 5 children: Leon Polk (HD5056, 1869-1877 d. age 8), Frank Lyon Polk (HD5047, first U.S. Under Secretary of State), Dr. John Metcalfe Polk Sr (HD5055, 1875-1904 d. autopsy pneumonia age 28), Serena Devereux Polk (HD5057, 1877-1878 d. infancy), Infant Daughter Polk (HD5058, b./d. unknown).
Second marriage after Ida's 1912 death. No children. Maria outlived him by 23 years.

notable.notable_blurb: Cornell Medical College Dean; Confederate Captain

military_service.role: Served in the Confederate Army under his father Lt. Gen. Leonidas Polk; advanced from cadet to captain.
Volunteered in 1917 at age seventy-two; held commission until death June 23, 1918.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote authoritative biography of his Confederate-general father
narrative_blocks.body: William Mecklenburg Polk wrote the two-volume Leonidas Polk: Bishop and General (1893), the standard biography of his father and a primary source for subsequent Polk scholarship. The work combined personal recollection with his father's papers and contemporary military records. It remained the definitive treatment of Bishop Polk well into the twentieth century.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Frank served as first US Under Secretary
narrative_blocks.body: His son Frank Lyon Polk served as Counselor to the U.S. Department of State through the First World War and became the first Under Secretary of State when that position was created in 1919. Frank had earlier served as Acting Secretary of State during portions of the Wilson administration. The Polk lineage — Bishop, then physician-biographer, then diplomat — spans three generations and three callings.

narrative_blocks.header: Cornell Medical College Dean and Gynecology Professor
narrative_blocks.body: After Confederate Army service, William Mecklenburg Polk graduated from Columbia's College of Physicians and Surgeons. He became Dean and Professor of Gynecology at Cornell University Medical College in 1898. Earlier appointments included Professor of Obstetrics and Gynecology at NYU (1879-1898) and Professor of Therapeutics and Clinical Medicine at Bellevue Hospital Medical College (1875-1879).

narrative_blocks.header: Returned to US service in WWI at seventy-two
narrative_blocks.body: Polk served as President of the New York Academy of Medicine from 1910 to 1914, and as President of the Aztec Club of 1847 in 1912-1913. After volunteering in 1917 at age seventy-two, he held a 1st Lieutenant commission in the U.S. Army Reserve Medical Corps until his death June 23, 1918 — a Confederate Captain who returned to U.S. service late in life.

marriages.notes: Frank Lyon Polk (HD5047) named for his mother Ida Lyon; John Metcalfe Polk (HD5055) born in his mother's hometown Demopolis AL.

parents.father_research_notes: Francis Strother Lyon (Demopolis AL) — not yet built as separate entry.
parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Serena Glover Lyon — not yet built as separate entry.

death.cause: killed in action by Union artillery

notable.notable_blurb: Bishop and Confederate general; Sewanee founder

institutions.institution_blurb: Principal organizing bishop and co-founder of the University of the South at Sewanee Tennessee; died June 1864 before the school opened.

military_service.notes: Commissioned 1861 despite no military service since West Point. Commanded corps at Shiloh, Perryville, Stones River, Chickamauga, Atlanta Campaign. Killed Pine Mountain GA Jun 14, 1864. Military historian Steven Woodworth called him 'one of the worst shots fired for the Union cause' — his incompetence as commander made him more valuable alive.

burial.plot_notes: Christ Church Cathedral, New Orleans, LA (reinterred 1945).

career.notes: One of the wealthiest slaveholding bishops in the United States. Owned Leighton Plantation, Thibodaux LA, worked by slaves brought from wife's NC plantations.
Founded the University of the South at Sewanee, Tennessee 1857 as an Episcopal university for the South. Construction interrupted by Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed by Union artillery on Pine Mountain, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Scouting with Generals Johnston and Hardee on Pine Mountain GA Jun 14, 1864, when Sherman spotted the officer cluster and ordered artillery. The third shell struck Polk in the left arm, went through his chest and exited — eyewitnesses said it 'nearly cut him in two.' Three identical books titled 'Balm for the Weary and Wounded' were found in his pocket, inscribed to Johnston, Hardee, and Hood and dated June 12, 1864 — two days before his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded the University of the South at Sewanee
narrative_blocks.body: He founded the University of the South at Sewanee, Tennessee in 1857 as a Southern Episcopal university — laying the cornerstone with bishops from ten dioceses. The Civil War halted construction; he never saw the institution open. The university finally welcomed its first students in 1868, four years after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Jefferson Davis's classmate and lifelong best friend
narrative_blocks.body: He and the future Confederate President were roommates and classmates at West Point, graduating together in 1827. The friendship persisted through Polk's resignation from the army for the church, through his bishopric, and into his Confederate generalship — Davis personally appointed him major general in 1861. Polk's funeral at New Orleans was attended by Davis and the Confederacy's top military and civil leadership.

sources.short: Online vital records; The Record LXXI; EH 1909

burial.plot_notes: Christ Church Cathedral, New Orleans, LA — reinterred 1945 with husband Bishop Leonidas Polk.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-great-granddaughter of Jonathan Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: Frances Ann Devereux Polk was the great-great-granddaughter of Jonathan Edwards (1703-1758) through the line Frances → her mother Frances Pollock Devereux → grandmother Eunice Edwards → great-grandfather Pierpont Edwards → Jonathan Edwards. The descent gave her family ties to one of America's most influential theological-philosophical figures and indirectly to Aaron Burr Sr.'s wife Esther Edwards, Jonathan Edwards's daughter. Her own marriage in 1830 carried the Edwards-Hooker line into the Episcopal-Confederate Polk family.

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War widow; outlived Bishop Polk eleven years
narrative_blocks.body: Frances and Bishop Leonidas Polk had married May 13, 1830 in Raleigh North Carolina, the year of his Episcopal ordination. On June 14, 1864 at Pine Mountain Georgia a Federal artillery shell killed him in the field — 'nearly cut in two' per battle reports — while he commanded a Confederate corps. Frances outlived him eleven years until her death April 17, 1875 in New Orleans, where she was buried at Christ Church Cathedral; both were reinterred together at Sewanee in 1945.

narrative_blocks.header: 1873-74 diary survives in UNC Southern Historical Collection
narrative_blocks.body: During her widowhood Frances kept a diary across 1873 and 1874 recording her activities at the Gale household near Nashville Tennessee, where her daughter Katherine Polk Gale and son-in-law William Dudley Gale lived. The diary survives in the Gale and Polk Family Papers at the Southern Historical Collection, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill — alongside Bishop Polk's Civil War letters, William Dudley Gale's Civil War letters as her son-in-law's aide-de-camp, and her daughter Katherine's 1895 Civil War recollections.

INST153.name: University of the South (Sewanee)

founding_year.notes: Chartered 1858; cornerstone laid 1860; effectively re-founded after Civil War destruction.

INST153.notes:
Founded 1856-1860 by Episcopal Church Southern dioceses, with Bishop Leonidas Polk (X00867) as the principal founder and one of the original 9 trustees. Polk envisioned a Southern Episcopal university; he died June 1864 before the school opened. The original campus was destroyed during the Civil War; the university opened in 1868. Polk's grandson Frank Lyon Polk (HD5047) was awarded D.C.L. honorary degree from the University of the South in 1928.
Connected people roles: X00867 = Co-founder, principal organizing bishop, original trustee. HD5047 = Awarded D.C.L. honorary degree 1928 (grandson of co-founder).
